summary: Gruber announces a new Preferences feature for Daring Fireball, addressing the site's most common complaint: body text that's too small due to pixel-based CSS font sizing that Internet Explorer won't let users resize. The font size switcher offers granular control from 10-16px rather than a simple small/medium/large choice. He also moves referrer listings behind a preference toggle, defaulting to off, as a countermeasure against referrer spammers trying to gain Google PageRank juice from DF's search engine reputation. The post closes with a self-deprecating pointer to an article pitting Gruber against Jakob Nielsen.
key_points: The #1 design complaint about DF was small body text, caused by pixel-based CSS sizing that IE (used by 90-95% of users) wouldn't let users resize
The font switcher uses a single dynamically-assembled PHP stylesheet rather than switching between multiple stylesheets
Referrer spammers exploit sites with public referrer listings to gain PageRank juice from search engines that spider those links
Hiding referrers by default doesn't prevent spam but guarantees spammers gain no SEO benefit from it
money_quotes: However, like any preferences system, over the course of the next few years it will expand to several hundred options.
One popular browser won't let users resize text specified using px; and that browser happens to be used by 90-95 percent of the world.
I don't think these scum bags have any interest or hope that Daring Fireball readers are particularly likely to click a link to 'gay-hitchhiker.top-porn-sites.net' (not made up).
This will have no preventive effect whatsoever to keep the spammers out, but it guarantees they won't benefit from their scum-bag behavior.

Humility almost, but not quite, prevents me from directing your attention to Andrei Herasimchuk's 'Gurus v. Bloggers', wherein yours truly is pitted against Jakob 'Two Wide Columns of Text Are Better Than One' Nielsen.
key_insight: Good web design means working around browser limitations your users are stuck with, and thoughtful defaults can neutralize spam without requiring any new technical defenses.
